---
title: "Euclid × SPT-3G CMB lensing"
subtitle: "The first Euclid (TR1) × SPT-3G κ cross-correlation"
occasion: SPT Summer Collaboration Meeting
description: First Euclid TR1 × SPT-3G CMB-lensing cross-correlation — a robust detection at a sub-unity amplitude
draft: true
author:
    name: Cail Daley
    email: cailmdaley@gmail.com
date: 2026-06-29
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"

output-file: index_v2.html

format:
    revealjs:
        width:  1920
        height: 1080
        theme: [default, custom.scss]
        slide-number: true
        fig-align: center
        include-after-body: assets/no_footer_on_titleslide.html
        footer: Cail Daley | Euclid × SPT-3G CMB lensing | SPT Summer Collaboration | June 2026
        history: false
        hash : true
        pdf-separate-fragments : true
        pdf-max-pages-per-slide	 : 1
        template-partials:
            - title-slide.html
        center-title-slide: false
image: images/spt26_footprint.png

html-math-method:
    method: mathjax
    url: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg-full.js
include-in-header: assets/mathjax.html
---

## Status report: Euclid × CMB-lensing cross-correlations {.center}

A status report on the **Euclid MoU CMB-lensing × cross-correlation project** —
led by **Cail Daley & Margherita Lembo**.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Today:**
footprints & fields · blinding · data vectors ·
estimator consistency · SPT vs ACT · likelihood & bias plans ·
systematics · simulations
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Timeline:**

- Euclid cosmology products now scheduled **June 2027**
- DR1 freeze slipped from **Oct 2026** [CONFIRM]
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is a status report, not a results talk — the detection and amplitude work is
blinded and in progress, so I'm reporting where the Euclid × CMB-lensing
cross-correlation project stands rather than headlining a number.

It's the Euclid MoU project, jointly led by me and Margherita Lembo, with Louis
Legrand and the rest of the 3×2pt+κ team.

Quick roadmap of where I'll go: the footprints and how we define the fields; our
blinding strategy; the data vectors — all three crosses; estimator consistency;
SPT versus ACT; then the forward-looking pieces — likelihood and bias plans,
systematics, and simulations.

On timing — and this matters for how we plan the MoU paper relative to Euclid: the
Euclid cosmology products are now scheduled for June 2027. That's the public
release / publication date set at the May consortium meeting; DR1 products are
final March 2027. This has slipped — the original DR1 freeze deadline was the
Oct-2026 red line, already missed in projection, mostly from shear-calibration
slippage. [CONFIRM the exact prior date before presenting — dictation was unclear,
and I want to be precise that the slip is freeze-Oct-2026 → publication-June-2027,
not muddle the freeze and publication dates.] The upshot the SPT side cares about:
we don't have to wait for June 2027 to publish — a paper can move on its own
schedule as long as what we publish matches what's frozen in the SAS.
:::

## Euclid footprints and overlaps

```{=html}
<div class="r-stack">
  <img src="images/spt26_footprint_1_euclid.png" style="width:84%">
  <img src="images/spt26_footprint_2_spt.png" class="fragment" style="width:84%">
  <img src="images/spt26_footprint_3_act.png" class="fragment" style="width:84%">
</div>
```

## Blinding: a standalone loose self-blind

![](images/spt26_selfblind_shift.png){width="74%"}

[A single secret amplitude factor shifts every spectrum by $\Delta C_\ell$ (blue);
**unblinded** (pink) vs **blinded** (green). Big enough to hide the answer
(**~23σ** across the data-vector diagonal), small enough to stay realistic
(**$S_8$ moves $\lesssim 1.5\sigma$**).]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
Quick word on blinding, because it shapes what I can and can't show you today.

Cross-correlations sit **outside the Euclid SGS blinding scope by
construction** — the official blinding acts on the catalog-level shear and
clustering, and the κ-crosses aren't covered. So rather than ride someone
else's scheme, we roll our **own** standalone, loose self-blind on the talk
timescale. It's deliberately simple: a **single secret amplitude factor** drawn
once, applied as a $\Delta C_\ell$ to the whole data vector — the blue line in
each panel.

The figure is just the honesty check that the blind does its job. Pink is the
true data, green is what I actually work with. Two things have to be true at
once. It has to be **big enough to hide the answer** — and it is: across the
data-vector diagonal the shift is about **23σ**, far more than enough that I
can't eyeball the amplitude. And it has to be **small enough to stay realistic**
— a blind that throws the data somewhere absurd lets you unblind by inspection.
Here it moves $S_8$ by only **a sigma to a sigma and a half**, so the blinded
vector still looks like a plausible universe.

The practical consequence for this talk: I've **dropped the amplitude and
detection-significance arc** entirely. Everything you'll see — the data vectors,
the estimator and survey consistency checks, the mocks — is built to be **read
under the blind**: shapes, relative consistency, error-bar sizes. The number
comes after we agree the pipeline is trustworthy and we unblind.
:::

## Data vectors: the three cross-correlations

![](images/spt26_cross_spectra.png){width="86%"}

[**All three crosses**, same NaMaster pseudo-$C_\ell$ bandpowers, $\ell C_\ell$:
$\gamma\times\kappa$ (shear × CMB-$\kappa$, top) and $\delta_g\times\kappa$ (clustering × CMB-$\kappa$, middle),
six tomographic bins each; $\delta_g\times\gamma$ (galaxy–galaxy lensing, bottom) shown as each clustering
bin × the deepest shear bin (the full 6×6 source×lens matrix is the backup). **No amplitude shown —
blinding-safe.**]{.credit style="font-size:0.6em"}

::: {.notes}
This is the data-vector slide — what we measure, not how it fits.

The spine for SPT is the two CMB-lensing crosses, top and middle: shear × kappa and galaxy clustering × kappa, six tomographic bins each, NaMaster pseudo-Cℓ bandpowers on the joint Euclid × SPT mask.

The bottom row is the third cross — galaxy–galaxy lensing, delta_g × gamma, Euclid internal, no CMB lensing. One wrinkle to flag honestly: GGL isn't a single-field cross like the kappa-crosses, it's a six-by-six source(shear)-by-lens(clustering) matrix. The broad Euclid tomographic n(z) overlap means there's signal in essentially every pair, but the cleanest, highest-S/N configuration is "source behind the lens." So on the slide I show the deepest shear bin — bin six, which sits behind all the clustering bins — crossed with each clustering lens bin: one clean spectrum per panel, parallel to the rows above. The full six-by-six matrix is a backup figure (spt26_ggl_matrix.png) if anyone wants it — and you can see the source-behind triangle is where the signal lives.

I'm deliberately not quoting amplitudes or per-bin S/N here — those belong to the detection arc, which is blinded and out of scope for this status report, and I've stripped them from the figure so nothing leaks. The message of this slide is just: three crosses, one consistent estimator, consistent tomographic binning, all tracking the Planck-18 shape. The figure carries the weight.
:::

## Robust to the SPT lensing estimator

![](images/spt26_estimator_comparison.png){width="82%"}

[Swapping the **GMV baseline** for the **bias + profile-hardened** reconstruction
moves every bandpower by **<1σ** (pull RMS 0.28σ), at no S/N cost — estimator-robust.
A foreground term *common* to both estimators cancels in the difference and is **not**
tested here; that is the reserved Websky/Agora recovery, not yet run.]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
This is a status check on whether the cross depends on which SPT lensing estimator we
feed it. The figure is gamma-kappa across the six tomographic bins; the same picture
holds for delta_g-kappa. Each panel overplots the GMV baseline against the bias- and
profile-hardened reconstruction, with a pull-residual strip underneath.

The message is one line: swapping estimators moves every bandpower by less than one
sigma — pull RMS 0.28 sigma in gamma-kappa, 0.29 in delta_g-kappa, all 120 bandpowers
inside 1 sigma, and the detection S/N doesn't drop. So the measurement is
estimator-robust.

Be careful here, and I want to be honest with this audience because you know lensing
biases cold: this is NOT a foreground-clean certificate. A tSZ or CIB contribution that
is common to GMV and the hardened estimator cancels in the difference — the hardening
correction being small bounds the *part that differs between estimators*, not the total
foreground bias. The direct test is a Websky or Agora-with-HOD recovery, which is
reserved and not yet run. So: estimator-robust, hardening correction bounded below one
sigma — and that's the claim, no more.
:::

## Two κ surveys on the same Euclid bins: SPT-3G and ACT DR6

![](images/spt26_cross_survey.png){width="92%"}

[Euclid RR2 × CMB-κ, six tomographic bins each: **shear×κ** (top), **clustering×κ** (bottom),
crossed against **SPT-3G GMV** (red) and **ACT DR6** (teal) over the fiducial theory. Two
independent κ reconstructions on the *same* southern bins.]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
This is the cross-survey check. Same Euclid tracers, same six tomographic bins, crossed
against two independent CMB-lensing reconstructions — SPT-3G GMV in red, ACT DR6 in teal —
both laid over the fiducial theory curve. Top row is shear×κ, bottom row is clustering×κ.

The message is just: we have two κ surveys over this southern overlap, and we can put both
through the identical pipeline on the identical bins. I'm deliberately *not* making an
amplitude or consistency claim here — that arc is blinded and in progress. The transferable
point is that the cross is reproducible across reconstructions, and that gives us a lever:
where SPT constrains better we lean on SPT, where ACT does we lean on ACT.

[SECOND ANGLE — now built; it's the next slide.] The direct "who constrains where" view
(`make_errorbar_compare.py`, σ pulled straight from the two on-disk covariances, theory as
the common reference, no amplitude shown) is the following slide. It **confirms the
crossover** I was hoping for: ACT tighter at large scales, SPT tighter at small scales — so
I can say that aloud.
:::

## Which κ survey constrains where

![](images/spt26_errorbar_compare.png){width="80%"}

[Per-bandpower error-bar size $\ell\sigma$ on the combined Euclid shear×κ data vector:
**SPT-3G GMV** (red) vs **ACT DR6** (teal), over the common fiducial theory $\ell C_\ell$.
Lower curve = tighter survey. σ read directly from each on-disk covariance — no amplitude
shown.]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
This is the "who constrains where" view — it answers the lever question from the previous
slide. I'm plotting the *size* of the error bar, ℓ·σ, for each survey on the same Euclid
shear×κ bins, with the common fiducial theory as the gray reference. The lower curve is the
tighter survey. It shows **no amplitude** — only the σ from each on-disk covariance — so it's
blinding-safe.

The story is a clean crossover, sitting between the ℓ=1141 and ℓ=1417 bandpowers (≈1350).
**ACT DR6 is tighter at large scales (ℓ ≲ 1350)** and **SPT-3G GMV is tighter at small scales
(ℓ ≳ 1350)**. That's exactly what you'd expect if
large scales are sample-variance limited (where ACT's wider Euclid overlap wins) and small
scales are reconstruction-noise limited (where SPT's depth and resolution win) — so the two
reconstructions are complementary, which motivates using both. The crossover is universal
across all six tomographic bins (I have a per-bin backup grid). Below the crossover the median
σ ratio ACT/SPT is ≈ 0.83 (ACT ~17% tighter); SPT pulls ~15–18% ahead at the top two
bandpowers (ℓ ≈ 2180, 2710).

One honest caveat to keep ready: the SPT covariance has two inflated low-ℓ bandpowers
(ℓ ≈ 162, and the known ℓ ≈ 84.5 spike) — visible as the red excursions — so don't over-read
the very lowest ℓ. The crossover conclusion is robust to them.
:::

## Likelihood & bias parameters — the plan

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](images/spt26_dd_joint_fit.png){width="100%"}

[Per-bin galaxy bias $b_i$ tightens when $\delta_g\delta_g$ joins $\delta_g\times\kappa$ — but the joint fit does **not** yet break the amplitude–bias degeneracy.]{.credit style="font-size:0.5em"}
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Being built**

- A **joint Gaussian likelihood** over the cross data vector — covariance from theory now, mocks next.

**How bias enters**

- Per-bin **$\{A,\, b_i\}$** fit — one nuisance $b_i$ per tomographic bin.
- Adding **$\delta_g\delta_g$** sharpens each $b_i$ — *bias-breaking power* — yet $A$ and $b$ stay degenerate in the cross alone.

::: {.fragment}
*Open:* what extra information (shear ratio? $\delta_g\delta_g$ scale range?) actually breaks $A\!-\!b$.
:::
:::
::::::::::::::

::: {.notes}
This is the most forward-looking slide — I want to be honest that much of the likelihood is still being designed, so read it as a plan, not a result.

What's being built: a joint Gaussian likelihood over the cross data vector. Right now the covariance is theory-based; the simulations slide that follows is partly in service of getting to a mock-based covariance.

How galaxy bias gets handled: we fit one amplitude A together with a per-tomographic-bin galaxy bias b_i — so a vector {A, b_1, ..., b_6}. The figure on the left makes the key structural point. The left panel: when you add the galaxy clustering auto-spectrum delta-g delta-g to the galaxy-lensing cross delta-g times kappa, each per-bin bias b_i tightens by roughly a factor of two and a half. So delta-g delta-g genuinely adds bias-breaking power — it pins the b_i much better.

But — and this is the honest limitation — that does NOT yet break the amplitude-bias degeneracy. In the cross-correlation A and b come in as a product, so even with delta-g delta-g sharpening the b_i, A and b are still degenerate along the same direction. I'm deliberately not putting amplitude numbers on this slide — that arc is blinded and dropped from this talk; the figure is here only to illustrate the bias-breaking point.

So the genuinely open design question, which I'd welcome thoughts on, is what additional information actually breaks A from b. Candidates: the shear-ratio geometry between tomographic bins, or pushing the delta-g delta-g scale range — but this is unsettled.

If asked about the figure's right and bottom panels — those carry amplitude and residual structure from the detection analysis; happy to discuss offline, but they're not the point of this slide.
:::

## Systematics: quantified for extinction, not yet closed

::: {.r-stack}
[NEW PLOT — systematics-null summary: $\sqrt{\sum_\ell (X/\sigma)^2}$ in $\sigma$ per
template × tomographic bin, one panel per cross ($\delta_g\times\kappa$, $\gamma\times\kappa$).
Extinction row populated; remaining template rows greyed "not yet redone."]{.placeholder style="font-size:0.7em; border:2px dashed #888; padding:2em 3em; display:inline-block;"}
:::

[Only **galactic extinction** is quantified on this catalog — coherent bias **~1–1.5σ aggregate**,
**sub-σ per bandpower** (DES/Chang $X = C^{\kappa S}C^{fS}/C^{SS}$). Stellar density, exposures,
zodiacal, N/S asymmetry: **not yet redone**. One template is not a systematics budget.]{.credit style="font-size:0.6em"}

::: {.notes}
This is a deliberately honest slide — I want to be clear about what we have and
haven't done on systematics, because it's the part of the analysis that's least
advanced.

The one cross-correlation systematic we've actually quantified on this catalog is
galactic extinction. The metric is the standard DES/Chang contamination estimate:
project the cross of each tracer with the extinction template, and the cross of
kappa with extinction, through the extinction auto — that gives the coherent bias
the template can inject into our signal. Per bandpower it's well sub-sigma, median
around a tenth of a sigma. The aggregate over ell, added in quadrature, comes out
around one to one-and-a-half sigma per bin — and it's common-mode, dominated by
the lowest ell bin around ell forty-four. So extinction is not a showstopper, but
it's not negligible either: it's at the level we'll have to carry as a correction
or a nuisance term, not wave away.

The placeholder is what this slide *should* become: a null-test summary — Δχ²
or this same bias metric in sigma — for every systematic template, across all the
tomographic bins, for both kappa crosses. Right now only the extinction row would
be filled in. Everything else — stellar density, exposure depth, zodiacal light,
PSF, the north-south survey asymmetry — those null tests exist from an earlier
catalog version but have not been redone on the catalog this analysis runs on, so
I'm not going to quote stale numbers as if they were current.

The transferable message is just that one line at the bottom: one template
quantified does not close systematics. This is the most open work-package in the
analysis, and I'd flag it as such rather than imply we've got systematics under
control. If anyone asks for the extinction figure specifically, I can show the
per-bin bar chart — it's real and current, June.
:::

## Simulations: GLASS mocks recover the input — except the high-ℓ aliasing rise

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}
![](images/spt26_glass_recovery.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="38%"}
- Known-truth **GLASS** δδ mocks, south, 6 tomo bins — recovery tracks input through the bulk of the range.
- The **high-ℓ rise above input** is **NGP gridding aliasing**, not signal: proven by a regrid test (vanishes at finer nside), consistent with Wolz+ 2024 App. B.
- Sets an **empirical scale cut, ℓ ≲ 865** (≤5% bias). Mechanism nailed; **no closed-form factor**.
:::
::::::::::::::

[Galaxy-clustering recovery vs GLASS input theory, 100 realizations.]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
This is the simulation leg — known-truth GLASS mocks, the same ones Arghavan Souki built to validate the VMPZ decontamination pipeline, run through our shared eDR1data pseudo-Cℓ engine. South footprint, six tomographic bins, 100 realizations. The point of the slide: does the pipeline recover a spectrum we put in?

Through the bulk of the range — out to a few hundred in ℓ — the recovered points sit right on the input theory. The one feature is the rise above the input curve at high ℓ, which you can see turning up in every bin in the last few bandpowers.

The headline is that we've pinned what that rise is. It is NGP gridding aliasing — the mocks are gridded with nearest-grid-point mass assignment, which folds super-Nyquist clustering power back down into the measured bands. And we didn't just infer that, we demonstrated it: regridding the same point catalogs at coarser and finer resolution, the excess collapses as a function of ℓ over the grid Nyquist, and it disagrees at fixed ℓ — at ℓ around 2700 the ratio to theory is 1.75 on the 1024 grid versus about 0.8 on a 2048 grid, hundreds of sigma apart. Measure finer and the excess goes away. That's the signature of an analysis-resolution effect, not intrinsic signal. It's consistent with what Wolz and collaborators saw in their 2024 appendix B.

The practical consequence: it sets an empirical scale cut for the galaxy-clustering autos, around ℓ of 865 for ≤5% bias. I want to be honest that the cut is empirical — the mechanism is nailed, but the simple NGP fold model underpredicts the near-Nyquist magnitude by about a factor of five, so there is no closed-form correction factor we trust. To push to higher ℓ you'd need an aliasing correction — interlacing or higher-order mass assignment — which is a question for the map builders.

If asked about the χ² numbers on the panels: those are dominated by exactly this high-ℓ aliasing, which is why they look terrible — that's the known pathology, and below the scale cut the recovery is clean. The cross-spectra that are the spine of this analysis carry no shot noise and are not where this bites; this is specifically about the clustering auto.
:::

## Summary & next steps {.center}

**Where we are** — Euclid TR1 × SPT-3G, a status report:

- Maps & fields defined; a standalone self-blind in place.
- All three cross data vectors measured: $\delta_g\times\gamma$, $\delta_g\times\kappa$, $\gamma\times\kappa$.
- Estimator- and cross-survey-consistent (SPT-3G GMV vs profile-hardened; SPT vs ACT).

::: {.fragment}
**Next** — joint $\{A, b_i\}$ likelihood · mock-based covariance · close systematics · DR1.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Thanks — Euclid CMBX SWG: **Margherita Lembo, Louis Legrand, Giulio Fabbian** and the 3×2pt+κ team — and the **SPT-3G lensing** team, whose winter GMV $\kappa$ makes this cross possible.
:::

::: {.notes}
Quick wrap. Where we are: the maps and field definitions are built and a self-blind is in place, so the analysis is running on a fixed, blinded footprint. We have all three cross data vectors in hand — galaxy–galaxy lensing, density–kappa, and shear–kappa. And the crosses are consistent two ways: across the SPT lensing estimator — GMV versus profile-hardened — and across surveys, SPT versus ACT. That's the status: the measurement infrastructure is in place and internally consistent.

What's next, and this is where the real work is: build the joint per-bin {A, b_i} likelihood; move to a mock-based covariance; close out systematics properly — extinction is the only one redone so far; and then DR1. (The gamma-gamma shear auto-spectrum, which was flagged as a broken spin-2 product earlier in the analysis, is now restored — the failure was a stale purified MCM-workspace cache, not an estimator problem, fixed and regression-guarded — so it's no longer a gap; it gives us the shear-calibration lever for the joint fit.)

Deliberately no amplitude number on this slide — that arc is blinded and in progress, so I'm reporting status, not a result.

Thanks to the Euclid CMBX SWG — Margherita Lembo, Louis Legrand, Giulio Fabbian and the 3×2pt+κ team — and to the SPT-3G lensing team, whose winter GMV kappa maps make this whole cross possible. Happy to take questions.
:::
